region_of_origin: China (Guangdong/Fujian); widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Yong Tau Foo as a composite hawker dish; closest parent match is stuffed tofu food product (FOODON_03311737). Composite dish with multiple stuffed items not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tofu and soy-based dishes — composite prepared dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Yong Tau Foo. A0FJP (tofu-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible parent anchor. Fried items and fish-paste stuffing are not individually coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Yong Tau Foo as a composite hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Yong Tau Foo with clear soup consists of approximately 7 mixed stuffed items (tofu, vegetables, tau pok) totalling about 400 g including broth.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional value of common hawker foods in Singapore — Yong Tau Foo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore hawker food nutritional database for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup, fried pieces). Per-100g values preserved from HPB-derived input record with confidence 0.95 for core macros.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate for tofu and non-starchy vegetable composite dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup, fried). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for tofu-based dishes and non-starchy vegetables (GI range 15–45). The high protein and fibre content from fish paste, tofu, and vegetables supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 22.0) / 100 = 7.7 per 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (280 mg per 100 g; ~1120 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit broth consumption and avoid dipping sauces to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day limit; choosing blanched over fried pieces further reduces sodium and fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (5.5 g per 100 g) with high protein (6.8 g) and dietary fibre (1.2 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yong Tau Foo with clear soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, high protein from fish paste and tofu, and fibre from vegetables; patients should avoid sweet bean sauce accompaniments and limit rice or noodle additions to control postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (4.2 g per 100 g) from deep-fried pieces; sodium load from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, select blanched rather than fried Yong Tau Foo pieces to reduce saturated fat intake, and minimise broth and sauce consumption to limit sodium to cardioprotective levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory nutrients from tofu, bitter gourd, and fish paste (omega-3 precursors)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tofu and non-starchy vegetables in Yong Tau Foo provide anti-inflammatory phytonutrients; deep-fried pieces should be avoided as oxidised fats may exacerbate inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo (酿豆腐 / 釀豆腐) is a Hakka Chinese dish that has become a defining staple of Singaporean and Malaysian hawker culture. The dish centres on a selection of items — typically firm tofu, fried tofu puffs (tau pok), bitter gourd, eggplant, okra, and chili peppers — each stuffed with a savoury fish paste (surimi) that is sometimes blended with minced pork. In this specific variant, 7 pieces are deep-fried and served in a clear soybean-based broth, yielding a dish that is simultaneously crispy (from frying) and light (from the clear soup). The flavour profile is mild and savoury, with the fish paste providing umami depth and the vegetables contributing slight bitterness and earthiness. Nutritionally, a 400 g serving provides approximately 340 kcal, 27 g protein, 16.8 g fat, and 22 g carbohydrate, with a low glycemic index of approximately 35, making it one of the more balanced hawker options. The dish is high in soy-derived protein and contains meaningful amounts of calcium, iron, vitamin A, and B vitamins from the fish paste and vegetables. Sodium content is moderate to high (approximately 1120 mg per serving including broth), which is a key consideration for hypertensive patients. For Type 2 Diabetes management, the low GI and high protein-to-carbohydrate ratio make this dish suitable when sweet sauces and starchy accompaniments are avoided. For cardiovascular health, choosing blanched over fried pieces and limiting broth intake reduces saturated fat and sodium exposure. The dish is customisable, halal-friendly, and dairy-free, and is commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, and dinner across Singapore and Malaysia.

What is the difference between fried and blanched Yong Tau Foo?
How much protein is in Yong Tau Foo?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced directly from the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) hawker food nutritional database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Input record carries confidence 0.95 for core macros and 0.90 for micronutrients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this composite dish.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g (1.1 g per 100 g) is estimated from HPB data for similar fried tofu and fish-paste dishes; the input record did not provide a discrete saturated fat value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite hawker preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; this is due to limited published data for traditional preparation of this complex composite dish. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as the HPB source reports negligible vitamin D for this dish; this is consistent with the absence of significant vitamin D sources in the ingredient profile. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for tofu-based and non-starchy vegetable composite dishes; no direct GI measurement for Yong Tau Foo (clear soup, fried) was found in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (35 × 22.0) / 100 = 7.7. 5. The dietary_tags in the input include 'vegetarian' and 'keto'; these have been removed from dietary_flags as the dish contains fish paste (surimi) and is not strictly vegetarian, and the fat-to-carbohydrate ratio does not meet standard ketogenic thresholds. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for Yong Tau Foo as a composite hawker dish.
